symptomSummary: After a prompt revision accumulates ~30+ invocations, the evolve loop ignores recent failures and optimizes against older feedback, producing regressions.
rootCauseHypothesis: The `forRevision` repository method sorts invocations by date ascending (oldest first) before applying a LIMIT, so once invocation count exceeds the telemetry window (default 25) the query silently returns only the oldest records, excluding all recent feedback and newly flagged failures from the evolve input.
fixHypothesis: Change the `orderBy` in `forRevision` from `asc(schema.invocations.date)` to `desc(schema.invocations.date)` so the query fetches the most-recent N invocations, matching the behavior already used by the sibling `forArtifact` method.

primaryLocation.rationale: The method orders by `asc(invocations.date)` then applies `LIMIT 25`. With fewer than 26 invocations every record is returned and evolve works correctly. Once a revision crosses that threshold the oldest 25 rows are returned instead of the newest 25, causing evolve to see only stale telemetry. The sibling method `forArtifact` (lines 125-132) already uses `desc(date)` for exactly this reason — `forRevision` was left with the wrong sort direction.

candidateLocations.rationale: The `evolve` function owns `telemetryWindow` (default 25) and passes it to `store.invocationsForRevision`. The `failing` filter on line 208 means that if recent failures are absent from the returned slice, evolve silently falls back to `recent[0]` (the oldest exemplar), compounding the stale-data problem.

The KV store is an alternate backend. If it also iterates invocations in insertion order without recency-capping it will exhibit the same regression once volume grows, independently of the SQLite fix.
The store interface may define an ordering contract. Confirming the interface aligns with a recency-first guarantee prevents custom adapters from reintroducing the ascending-order bug.

Entry point for UI-triggered evolve calls. If it forwards a caller-supplied `telemetryWindow` without validation, a client could pass an oversized value that amplifies the stale-data exposure even after the sort-order fix.
